translations.translation:
Liao Li (Gongyuan)
廖立 (公淵)
Lived ADca.178–ca.246
aSanguozhiBiographyRecords of the Three Kingdoms [三國志]
byZL181
onApril 12th, 2023
No History Reference defined for Officer ID '329' Liao Li (Gongyuan). Biography cannot be tied to a scroll in the Sanguozhi.

Liao Li,AstyledGongyuan, was fromLinyuan[臨沅],Wuling[武陵].
Liao [廖] has the fanqie of Li [理] and Jiu [救].
When theFormer Lordwas serving asGovernorofJing province[荊州牧], he was recruited to serve asClerk. He was not even thirty years old. He was then appointed to serve asGrand AdministratorofChangsha[長沙太守].
When theFormer Lordentered Shu [蜀],Zhuge Lianggarrisoned the lands ofJing[荊]. WhenSun Quansent an envoy to foster friendship with[Zhuge] Liang, he asked which scholars could plan and govern.[Zhuge] Lianganswered, “Pang TongandLiao Liare good talents from Chu [楚]. They will help with bringing prosperity to the world.”
In the twentieth year of theera [215],[Sun] QuansentLü Mengto suddenly attack the three commanderies in the south.[Liao] Liescaped and fled, returning to theFormer Lord. TheFormer Lordhad always known him and treated him well, so he did not blame him too deeply. He was madeGrand AdministratorofBa commandery[巴郡太守].
In the twenty-fourth year [219], when theFormer Lordbecame King ofHanzhong[漢中王], he summoned[Liao] Lito serve asPalace Attendant.
After theLatter Lordsucceeded the throne, he was shifted to serve asCommandant of the Chang River Encampments. In the base thoughts of[Liao] Li, he thought that his own talent and reputation should be second toZhuge Liang. As he was shifted to an idle [role] and ranked below the likes ofLi Yan, he often felt unhappy. Later, when thebureaucratsunder theAssisting Chancellor,Li ShaoandJiang Wan, came,[Liao] Ligave his idea:
“As the army is going to march out afar, you gentlemen should ruminate over such a matter. In the times of yore, thedeparted emperordid not takeHanzhong[漢中], but went to fight with the people ofWufor the three commanderies of the south. In the end, the three commanderies were given to the people ofWu. That was an useless effort for the officials and soldiers, and they returned with no gains. WhenHanzhong[漢中] was lost, this allowed forXiahou YuanandZhang Heto penetrate intoBa[巴] and almost seize the province. Later, when we arrived atHanzhong[漢中], this allowed forMarquis Guanto perish without any survivors left behind. Even more,Shangyong[上庸] was seized, and we lost another piece of land. That[Guan] Yurelied on his valorous reputation and commanded armies without any organization was due to his whims. Hence, several battalions perished throughout his tenure. The likes ofXiang LangandWen Gongare ordinary men, nothing more.[Wen] Gongserves asOfficial of the Government Seat, but he lacks any governance.[Xiang] Langhad reveredMa Liangand his brothers in the past, claiming they were sages, and now he serves asChief Clerk. He has always been capable of conforming to the flow.Household GentlemanGuoYanchangis a follower and nothing more than that. He is not worthy of being involved in planning largescale matters, yet he has been madePalace Attendant. Now in this declining era, it is unbefitting to have wanted to appoint these three people to office!Wang Lianfollows vulgar trends and callously makes ways to extract money, thereby causing the common people to become impoverished and exhausted. These have resulted in our current situation!”
[Li] Shaoand[Jiang] Wanreported such words toZhuge Liang.[Zhuge] Liangpetitioned about[Liao] Li, stating:
“TheCommandant of the Chang River Encampments,Liao Li, professes himself to be grandiose and important, making critical comments on many gentlemen. He openly declared that the empire had not appointed the virtuous and talented, but had appointed mediocre officials, and he also said the military commanders of hosts tens of thousands strong were all rascals. He has defamed thedeparted emperorand slandered our officials! Whenever people said that the soldiers of the empire were concise in their training and precise in their organization,[Liao] Liraised his head and looked over the room, then, with an annoyed face, said, ‘Why even mention such?’ The number of times he was like this cannot be counted. If even one sheep can agitate the flock and still do harm, what more is there to say of[Liao] Li, who is reliant on his high position? Can average people recognize his truths andlies?”B
TheCollection of Zhuge Liangcontains the petition of[Zhuge] Liang, which read:
“[Liao] Liserved thedeparted emperorwithout a shred of loyalty or piety. When he governedChangsha[長沙], he opened its gates to the enemy; when he governedBa commandery[巴郡], he was foolish and handled matters in an ignoble manner; when he followed the grand army, he made slanderous and sardonic remarks; and when he waited next to the imperial coffin, he unsheathed his sword and beheaded someone next to the imperial coffin. AfterYour Majestyascended the throne, everyone was given ranks and titles; the role[Liao] Lihad was likened to being a General. However, he spoke face-to-face with me, saying, ‘How is it appropriate for me to be placed among generals? You did not petition for me to serve as aMinister, but memorialized for me to be one of the Five Colonels instead!’ And I, your servant, replied, ‘The role of General is the highest one pertaining to you. As for the role ofMinister,Zhengfanghas also not yet been made such; moreover, it is appropriate to place you among the Five Colonels.’ From that moment onward, he harbored discontent.”
An edict stated:
“When the Sanmiao people rebelled against the government,Yumercifully exiled them.1Liao Liis mad and confused, and I cannot bear to punish him, so I shall only force him into exile in barrenlands.”•••
The first emperor of theXia dynasty,Emperor Yu, waged war on the powerful Sanmiao people because they refused to accept his authority and send tribute. They had previously refused to acceptEmperor Shunas their sovereign.Emperor Yudefeated them in a largescale battle.↩
Thus,[Liao] Liwas deposed, made a commoner, and exiled toWenshan commandery[汶山郡].[Liao] Lipersonally led his wife and children to plow and farm so as to support themselves. When he heard thatZhuge Liangdied, he cried tears and sighed, saying, “I am fated to be an outsider2until the end!” Later, whenArmy SupervisorJiang Weiled a secondary army throughWenshan[汶山], he went to[Liao] Li. He praised that[Liao] Lihad not declined in demeanor and spirit, speaking and discussing as he had always.[Liao] Lithus perished in his place of exile. His wife and children returned to Shu [蜀].•••
Here, the literal term of “outsider” is “lapel on the left” [左袵]. Foreigners, barbarians, or outsiders often fastened their robes towards their left side.↩
